title: Product-Market Fit Evidence

introduction.subtitle: Evidence maturity assessment. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
Product-Market Fit Evidence helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Retention & Behaviour, Demand Pull & Willingness to Pay, Segment Clarity, Repeatable Acquisition. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: What does retention look like for the most relevant customer cohort?

options.text: A. The answer is broad, assumed, or based mostly on internal belief.
B. There is a high-level description, but segmentation, trigger, or buyer evidence is thin.
C. The answer is defined for a specific segment with documented behaviour, alternatives, and decision criteria.
D. The answer is validated across repeated cases and directly informs targeting, messaging, prioritisation, and trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Retention & Behaviour: What does retention look like for the most relevant customer cohort
conceptHelp.summary: Retention & Behaviour means whether target customers keep using the product in a recurring workflow or outcome that proves continuing value. This question narrows the concept to "What does retention look like for the most relevant customer cohort?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Retention & Behaviour matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would trace "What does retention look like for the most relevant customer cohort?" to customer behavior, product data, ownership, and a roadmap or go-to-market decision that changed because of it.

questions.text: How central is the product to the customer recurring workflow or outcome?

options.text: A. The answer is broad, assumed, or based mostly on internal belief.

Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: What evidence demonstrates willingness to pay or expand?

options.text: A. The answer relies mainly on opinion, pitch, memory, or isolated examples.
B. There are supporting examples, but sources are partial, informal, or hard to compare.
C. There is documented evidence from relevant customers, operations, metrics, or records.
D. The evidence is triangulated, current, owner-reviewed, and strong enough to change a material decision.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Demand Pull & Willingness to Pay: What evidence demonstrates willingness to pay or expand
conceptHelp.summary: Demand Pull & Willingness to Pay means evidence that customers actively seek, pay for, expand, or advocate for the product without constant founder persuasion. This question narrows the concept to "What evidence demonstrates willingness to pay or expand?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Demand Pull & Willingness to Pay matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would trace "What evidence demonstrates willingness to pay or expand?" to customer behavior, product data, ownership, and a roadmap or go-to-market decision that changed because of it.

conceptHelp.summary: Segment Clarity means knowing which customer segment shows the strongest fit and what use case, buyer, and trigger define it. questions.text: How repeatable are customer acquisition and conversion?

options.text: A. The answer is broad, assumed, or based mostly on internal belief.
B. There is a high-level description, but segmentation, trigger, or buyer evidence is thin.
C. The answer is defined for a specific segment with documented behaviour, alternatives, and decision criteria.
D. The answer is validated across repeated cases and directly informs targeting, messaging, prioritisation, and trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Repeatable Acquisition: How repeatable are customer acquisition and conversion
conceptHelp.summary: Repeatable Acquisition means whether the company can acquire and convert similar customers repeatedly with understood channels, costs, and failure reasons. This question narrows the concept to "How repeatable are customer acquisition and conversion?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Repeatable Acquisition matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would trace "How repeatable are customer acquisition and conversion?" to customer behavior, product data, ownership, and a roadmap or go-to-market decision that changed because of it.

questions.text: How confidently can you identify the cohort where retention stabilises?

options.text: A. No concrete example shows the cohort where retention stabilises; the answer relies on intention, memory, or individual judgement.
B. There are isolated examples of the cohort where retention stabilises, but they are inconsistent, hard to verify, or depend on one person.
C. Recent examples show the cohort where retention stabilises in practice, with enough detail to judge quality and repeatability.
D. The cohort where retention stabilises is repeatedly demonstrated under pressure and changes decisions, ownership, or trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Retention & Behaviour: How confidently can you identify the cohort where retention stabilises
conceptHelp.summary: Retention & Behaviour is tested here through a concrete operating question: "How confidently can you identify the cohort where retention stabilises?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Retention & Behaviour matters because weak practice in this area can make the whole result look more mature than the operating system really is.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable behaviour: artifacts, decisions, customer evidence, owners, reviews, or shipped work. Do not score intention as practice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
